section.title: The Death of Abraham
section.sectionRationale: Abraham's long life comes to its quiet, contented close. He settles his other children with gifts, gives all he has to Isaac, and dies full of years. Two estranged sons bury him together, and God's blessing passes on to Isaac, the promise still moving.
section.thesis: Abraham dies old and satisfied, his inheritance secured for Isaac and his blessing continuing; the man who lived by promise is gathered to his people in peace, and the covenant blessing flows on to the next generation.
section.frameNote: Three scenes: Abraham's other children provided for; the death and burial of Abraham; God's blessing passing to Isaac.

units.label: Abraham's other children
units.note: gifts to the sons of the concubines

verses.text: Now Abraham had taken another wife, named Keturah,
and she bore him Zimran, Jokshan, Medan, Midian, Ishbak, and Shuah.
Jokshan was the father of Sheba and Dedan. And the sons of Dedan were the Asshurites, the Letushites, and the Leummites.
The sons of Midian were Ephah, Epher, Hanoch, Abida, and Eldaah. All these were descendants of Keturah.
Abraham left everything he owned to Isaac.
But while he was still alive, Abraham gave gifts to the sons of his concubines and sent them away from his son Isaac to the land of the east.

units.label: The death and burial of Abraham
units.note: old and contented, gathered to his people

verses.text: Abraham lived a total of 175 years.
And at a ripe old age he breathed his last and died, old and {{contented|contented}}, and was {{gathered|gathered to his people}}.
His sons Isaac and Ishmael buried him in the cave of Machpelah near Mamre, in the field of Ephron son of Zohar the Hittite.
This was the field that Abraham had bought from the Hittites. Abraham was buried there with his wife Sarah.
After Abraham’s death, God {{barak|blessed}} his son Isaac, who lived near Beer-lahai-roi.

contented.gloss: Full, satisfied, having had enough. It describes a life lived to fullness and a soul at rest, not merely long years. Abraham dies satisfied, having seen God begin to keep His promises, a man who received the son of promise and could let go in peace.

gathered.gloss: To gather, collect, or take in. The phrase gathered to his people speaks of joining one's forefathers in death, and hints at a hope beyond the grave, that the dead are not lost but gathered. It is used of the peaceful passing of the patriarchs into the company of those who went before.

barak.gloss: To bless, to bestow favour and life giving good, from a root linked to kneeling. God's blessing is His active goodness that makes fruitful and prospers. Here the blessing that rested on Abraham passes to Isaac, so the covenant favour continues unbroken into the next generation.

basic.context: Abraham's story draws to a close. He has other sons through Keturah, but he provides for them with gifts and sends them east, while giving his whole inheritance to Isaac, the son of promise. He dies at 175, old and satisfied, and is buried by Isaac and Ishmael. The key question is how the promise continues past Abraham's death.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a historical summary that closes one era and opens another. It carefully distinguishes Isaac, the heir of the covenant, from Abraham's other children. The question is how the writer signals that God's promise is not tied to Abraham's life but flows on to Isaac by God's own choice and blessing.
basic.originalLanguages: Abraham dies sabea, full and contented, a life brought to satisfying completeness. He is gathered, asaph, to his people, a phrase hinting at hope beyond death. Then God blesses, barak, Isaac, so the covenant favour that rested on the father continues on the son.
basic.typology: Abraham dying in faith, satisfied though he saw only the first fruits of the promise, pictures all who die trusting God's word. The blessing passing to Isaac foreshadows the inheritance that comes to God's children in Christ, the true heir through whom the blessing reaches the world.
basic.christ: The blessing that flows from Abraham to Isaac runs on through the generations to Christ, in whom the promise to bless all nations is fulfilled. Abraham died satisfied by faith in what he could not yet fully see; in Christ the promise he trusted is brought to completion.
basic.god: God is faithful across a whole lifetime and beyond it. He blessed Abraham in every way, gave him a satisfying end, and then blessed Isaac, showing that His promise does not die with the patriarch. He is the God who gathers His people and carries His covenant forward.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 15 promised Abraham he would go to his fathers in peace at a good old age. Hebrews 11 says he died in faith. Galatians 3 says the blessing of Abraham comes to the nations in Christ. Matthew 22 calls God the God of the living.
basic.oneStory: Abraham dies full of years and faith, and the blessing passes to Isaac. The promise is bigger than any one life; it moves on through the covenant line, generation to generation, toward Christ, in whom the whole world is blessed.

context.literary: A closing summary for the Abraham narrative, listing his later offspring, recording his death and burial, and handing the story to Isaac. It rounds off the toledot structure of Genesis and forms a bridge to the accounts of Ishmael and of Jacob and Esau that follow.
context.historical: Providing for secondary sons with gifts while granting the main inheritance to the chosen heir reflects ancient family practice. Sending the other sons eastward accounts for peoples such as the Midianites, situating Israel among its neighbours.
context.canonical: Abraham's peaceful death fulfils the earlier promise of Genesis 15:15. The burial in the cave he purchased ties back to chapter 23 and keeps the land promise in view, while the blessing on Isaac carries the covenant toward its later heirs and, ultimately, Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To bring Abraham's life to a fitting close, to distinguish Isaac clearly as the covenant heir, and to show that God's blessing continues past Abraham's death by resting on his son.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes how Abraham settled his affairs and died; it does not prescribe inheritance customs. Its abiding message is the faithfulness of God across generations and the peace of a life lived and ended in faith.

debates.question: What does the phrase gathered to his people imply about life after death?

views.label: A hint of hope beyond the grave
views.case: The phrase is distinct from being buried and suggests joining one's forebears, an early, seed form of hope that the dead are not simply gone but gathered.

views.label: A dignified idiom for dying
views.case: The expression may be mainly a respectful way of describing a peaceful death and burial among one's kin, without a developed doctrine of the afterlife.

debates.bearing: Either way it frames Abraham's death as peaceful and hopeful, and the New Testament, calling God the God of the living, reads such hope as real and grounded in the resurrection.

study.originalLanguages: Three words carry the weight. Abraham dies sabea, full and satisfied, a life brought to completeness rather than merely a long span. He is gathered, asaph, to his people, an expression that gently reaches beyond the grave. And after his death God barak, blesses, Isaac, the same word used of God's favour on Abraham, signalling that the covenant blessing has not ended but passed to the son.

typology.type: Abraham dying satisfied though he saw only the promise's beginnings
typology.fulfillment: The faith of all who die trusting God's word, welcoming the promises from afar, Hebrews 11:13.

typology.type: The blessing passing from Abraham to Isaac
typology.fulfillment: The blessing of Abraham reaching the nations in Christ, the true heir, Galatians 3:14.

study.christ: The blessing that God transfers from Abraham to Isaac keeps moving down the covenant line until it reaches Christ, in whom the promise to bless all the families of the earth comes true. Abraham died satisfied by faith, having seen only the dawn of what God pledged; the full day arrives in Jesus. And Abraham gathered to his people, resting in hope, belongs among those of whom Jesus says God is not the God of the dead but of the living, a hope secured by the resurrection of Christ.
study.god: God proves faithful over an entire lifetime and past its end. He blessed Abraham in every way, granted him a satisfying and peaceful death, and then blessed Isaac, so that the promise does not die with the patriarch. He is the God who gathers His people and carries His covenant forward, generation after generation.

crossReferences.note: God's earlier promise that Abraham would go to his fathers in peace and be buried at a good old age, fulfilled here.
Abraham died in faith, not having received all the promises but greeting them from afar.
The blessing of Abraham comes to the Gentiles in Christ Jesus, the goal of the blessing passed to Isaac.
God is the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, the God of the living, grounding hope beyond the grave.

study.oneStory: Abraham, the man who staked everything on God's promise, dies old and satisfied, and God's blessing flows on to Isaac. The story shows that the promise is greater than any single life; it moves through the covenant line, carried by God's faithfulness toward Christ, in whom every nation is blessed.

quiz.q: How did Abraham provide for his sons other than Isaac?

options.text: He gave them gifts and sent them away eastward, while leaving all his inheritance to Isaac.
options.note: Verses 5 and 6 distinguish the gifts to the other sons from the inheritance given to Isaac.

options.text: He divided his estate equally among all his sons.
options.note: He provides for them, but the inheritance goes to Isaac, not an equal division.

options.text: He left them nothing at all.
options.note: He does give them gifts; they are not left with nothing.

options.text: He sent them to live with Ishmael.
options.note: He sends them to the land of the east, not to Ishmael.

quiz.q: How does the text describe Abraham's death?

options.text: He died at a ripe old age, old and contented, and was gathered to his people.
options.note: Verse 8 pictures a full, peaceful, and satisfied death.

options.text: He died suddenly and unprepared.
options.note: The opposite; his death is peaceful and his affairs are settled.

options.text: He died grieving that he had seen none of God's promises.
options.note: He dies contented, having seen the promise begin in Isaac.

options.text: He died in battle defending his household.
options.note: There is no battle; he dies at a great age in peace.

options.text: Isaac and Ishmael together, in the cave of Machpelah he had bought.
options.note: Verses 9 and 10 record the two sons burying him in the purchased field.

options.text: Isaac alone, in a new tomb near Beersheba.
options.note: Isaac is involved, but Ishmael joins him, and the site is Machpelah, not a new tomb.

options.text: The Hittites, in one of their own tombs.
options.note: He is buried by his sons in the field he purchased from the Hittites, not in their tombs.

options.text: Keturah and her sons, in the land of the east.
options.note: It is Isaac and Ishmael who bury him, at Machpelah.

quiz.q: What happens to God's blessing after Abraham dies?

options.text: God blesses Isaac, so the covenant favour continues to the next generation.
options.note: Verse 11 shows the blessing passing on; the promise does not die with Abraham.

options.text: The blessing ends with Abraham's death.
options.note: The opposite; the text stresses that God then blessed Isaac.

options.text: The blessing is divided among all Abraham's sons.
options.note: The blessing rests on Isaac specifically, not on all the sons.

options.text: The blessing passes to Ishmael as the elder son.
options.note: God blesses Isaac, the son of promise, not Ishmael.

quiz.q: What does Abraham's contented death teach about living by God's promise?

options.text: One can die satisfied by faith even having seen only the beginnings of what God pledged.
options.note: Abraham rests in God's faithfulness though full fulfilment lay ahead.

options.text: God's promises must be fully seen in this life or faith fails.
options.note: Abraham died before full fulfilment yet contented; faith rests in God, not in complete sight.

options.text: A long life is proof that one has pleased God.
options.note: His contentment rests on God's faithfulness, not merely on length of days.

options.text: The promise depended on Abraham staying alive.
options.note: The blessing passes to Isaac; the promise outlasts Abraham's life.

questions: Abraham died old and contented, at peace with God and his family. What would it take for you to be able to say your life is satisfied in God?
The inheritance went to Isaac, the son of promise, by God's design. Where do you need to trust God's choices even when they differ from human expectation?
Two estranged brothers, Isaac and Ishmael, buried their father together. Is there a broken relationship where God might be inviting you toward peace?
God's blessing did not die with Abraham but passed to Isaac. How does it encourage you that God's purposes are bigger than any single life, including your own?
Abraham was gathered to his people in hope. How does the resurrection of Christ shape the way you think about your own death and those you have lost?

translationNotes.note: The phrase gathered to his people is distinct from being buried in the next verse, and suggests joining one's forebears, an early hint of hope beyond death.
The sons of the concubines refers to children of Keturah and Hagar; they are provided for but set apart from Isaac, the covenant heir.
